What Is a Promise in JavaScript? And Why You Really Need to Get It

This post walks through what a Promise actually is in JavaScript, plus the Async/Await syntax that builds on top of it. We'll cover how to write a Promise, the then/catch chain, the static methods like Promise.all and Promise.race, the three Promise states, and how async/await cleans all of it up into something far easier to read.
A Field Guide to JavaScript Error Types (So You Can Debug Faster)

A quick reference for the different kinds of errors JavaScript throws at you. We stare at red error messages all day during development, so if you can recognize the error type at a glance, it actually makes debugging a lot easier. This post walks through SyntaxError, RangeError, ReferenceError, TypeError, and URIError with real examples.
JavaScript's Event Loop, Event Queue, Event Table and Event Task — Explained Simply

I was reading through some docs and ran into a detailed explanation of the Event Queue, so I figured I'd write it down. This post walks through what the Event Queue, Event Table, Event Loop, and Event Task (MacroTask vs MicroTask) actually are in JavaScript, and why your async code runs in an order you might not expect.
JS Core (72) - ES6 Tagged Template Literals: The Backtick Trick You've Never Used

Template literals are the backtick strings you use every day. But there's a more advanced form hiding in there — tagged template literals. This post walks through how they work, why the output looks so weird at first, and two practical uses: bulk-wrapping values in HTML and preventing XSS attacks.
JavaScript Core (71) - ES6 Template Literals: Nesting

Template literals can do more than simple string interpolation. In this post we dig into what kinds of things you can actually put inside `${}` — why expressions work but statements don't — and how nesting template literals lets you build DOM strings cleanly with map and join.
JavaScript Core (70) - ES6 Template Literals: Writing HTML with Emmet Inside Template Literals in VSCode

A quick one about Template Literals over in VSCode. If you write HTML with Emmet, you've probably noticed it doesn't fire inside template literals by default. Here's how to get Emmet expanding tags inside template strings so you can keep using those handy shortcuts.
JavaScript Core (69) - ES6: Template Literals - A Quick Intro to Template Literals

Template literals are one of those techniques you'll use constantly in modern JavaScript. This post walks through the basics — how they replace the old string-concatenation-with-plus approach, how to drop variables in with ${}, and a handy trick for setting default values when a variable comes back empty or undefined.
JavaScript Core Concepts (68) - ES6 Arrow Functions: Chapter Exercise

We've finally reached the end of the "ES6 Arrow Functions" chapter, so let's take a look at the exercise it has in store for us. Two short problems, both built around the same trap: arrow functions don't have their own `this`. If you can spot why each snippet breaks and rewrite it correctly, you've got this chapter down.
A Quick Trick for Resetting Vue Data to Initial Values
We often need to reset data to its initial values during development. This post is a quick tip for doing exactly that — and the trick works on both Vue 2 and Vue 3.
Arrow Functions in Practice: Where They Actually Shine (and Where to Stop)

Now that we've covered how arrow functions work, let's look at where you'll actually use them day to day. Mostly that means pairing them with array methods like map and reduce, where they really cut down the noise. We'll rewrite a couple of regular functions as arrow functions and see how much shorter they get — plus a quick warning about when not to over-shorten.
